headline: CORN NEARBY BREAKS 6%; OLD-CROP CARRY INVERTS
subheadline: Nearby corn fell hard while Dec '26 barely moved, blowing the carry structure wide open and leaving producers with a storage decision to make before pollination weather takes over the narrative.
lead: Nearby corn dropped 27 cents to $4.11 while Dec '26 barely twitched, closing at $4.38. That 27-cent spread between the nearby and new crop is the market telling you storage is not being rewarded right now; it is being penalized. Heat and humidity are moving into the Heartland over the next 6-to-10 days per this morning's weather outlook, and pollination is approaching. The question this week was whether Crop Progress data removed the last weather premium from corn. Today's break in the nearby says the premium was already gone, but the calendar is about to test that assumption.
the_takeaway: Corn's nearby broke hard; heat moves in next week. The weather premium is getting priced back in.
teaser: Nearby corn fell 27 cents while Dec barely moved. The carry structure just told you something important about storage.

one_number.context: Nearby corn closed $4.11 against Dec '26 at $4.38, a 27-cent carry with the nearby sitting 6.2% below yesterday's close while the new-crop contract barely moved. When the nearby breaks hard and new crop holds, the market is pricing a specific idea: old-crop supply is ample right now, but new-crop faces uncertainty. For producers holding bushels on farm, that spread is the cost of waiting. The carry is not working in your favor at these levels.

yesterdays_call.summary: Wheat broke cleanest; corn and beans followed on low conviction. Next leg requires a weather event or export surprise.
yesterdays_call.note: Miss. The call was looking for corn to follow wheat higher and needed a weather event or export catalyst to provide the next leg up. Instead corn provided the catalyst in the opposite direction, breaking 27 cents on old-crop supply weight. The setup read the weather premium as still present in corn; the market had already priced it out. Weather is now back on the table for new crop, but that does not rescue the prior call.

sections.title: NEARBY CORN BREAKS HARD
sections.body: Nearby corn fell **27¼ cents to $4.11**, a 6.2% single-session drop that ranks as the second hard break in three sessions. This is the overnight surprise of the day, magnitude 4.1x the threshold, and it needs a clear-eyed read. Dec '26 closed at $4.38, up less than a penny. That divergence is the story: the nearby is pricing the reality of a well-supplied old-crop pipeline while new crop holds because the Heartland weather outlook shifted overnight. The 6-to-10 day forecast now calls for above-normal heat and humidity along and east of a line from southwestern New Mexico to the Red River Valley of the North. Pollination is approaching. **The nearby broke because storage is not being rewarded; the new crop held because heat is on the calendar.** Producers still holding old-crop bushels on farm need to look at local basis today. If basis is firm in your area, the futures board just gave you a closing window.
sections.catalyst: Overnight weather shift: above-normal heat and humidity moving into Heartland over 6-10 days, pollination approaching.
sections.bottom_line: Old-crop broke hard; new crop held. That spread is pricing supply now versus weather risk next.
sections.farmer_action: Producers with on-farm grain: check local basis today. If basis is firm, the futures break has narrowed your window. Evaluate before the heat forecast gets priced further into the new-crop contract.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: yesterday's modest easing became a hard break on weather shift.

sections.title: BEANS AND WHEAT HOLD STEADY
sections.body: Soybeans nearby added 2¾ cents to $11.19 and Nov '26 matched at $11.45, both up 0.2%. Chicago wheat added the same 2¾ cents to $5.88. These markets did not follow corn lower, which is itself information. The American Soybean Association's CEO told Brownfield this morning he is encouraged by the Senate farm bill framework, calling it a necessary step toward a full five-year bill. That policy backdrop is supportive but not a futures driver today. **The separation between corn's break and beans' flat-to-firm close tells you this was a corn-specific event, not a broad grain selloff.** Soybean meal gained 1.1% to $304.60, which kept the complex supported. The nearby-to-November bean spread is flat, carry is not widening, and there is no urgency signal from the board on new crop. Watch the heat forecast: if it extends into the primary soybean belt, that story changes.
sections.catalyst: Corn-specific break; beans supported by meal strength at $304.60 and ASA's positive farm bill commentary.
sections.bottom_line: Beans and wheat held while corn broke. Meal strength carried the oilseed complex.
sections.vs_yesterday: Wheat: recovered a few cents after leading lower Tuesday. Beans: quietly firm, no drama.

sections.title: CATTLE WAIT ON DIRECT TRADE
sections.body: Live cattle settled at **$246.05, down 0.7%**. Feeders closed at $368.27, down 0.8%. The CME report from Brownfield this morning is plain: August live cattle closed $1.35 lower at $246, feeders lower as the market waits on the week's direct business. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler plant lockout, which began May 19, is still creating processing-constrained price dynamics rather than supply-constrained ones. The fed cattle trade has not reset since the last Cattle on Feed report, and there is nothing in today's box-beef cutout that forces a hand. **The market is patient, not broken.** The Asian Longhorned tick story out of Missouri is a longer-term herd management concern, not a today price driver, but it is worth tracking. Watch is still the right verb for cattle.
sections.catalyst: August live cattle closed $1.35 lower per CME; market awaiting week's direct trade. Cargill lockout processing constraint ongoing.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle easing into direct trade, not breaking. Processing constraint still the backdrop.

sections.title: ENERGY EASES; QATAR TIMELINE EMERGES
sections.body: WTI crude settled at **$72.08, down 0.5%**. Natural gas added 1.0% to $3.22. The Iran-Hormuz situation, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, got a parallel development today: Qatar's prime minister told the Financial Times that LNG exports could return to normal production within a few weeks. That is a supply-side signal for natural gas markets globally, and it partly explains why U.S. natural gas at $3.22 did not run harder on the domestic heat outlook. VLCC tanker earnings reportedly topped $470,000 per day as oil importers scrambled to charter vessels ahead of the Strait reopening, which tells you the physical market is moving faster than the futures strip. **Crude is range-bound; the Hormuz premium is unwinding on schedule.** China's teapot refineries cutting to their lowest run rates since 2017 is a demand-side headwind that the futures board is already pricing.
sections.catalyst: Qatar PM: LNG exports back to normal within weeks. China teapot refinery runs at lowest since 2017. Hormuz tanker frenzy ongoing.
sections.bottom_line: Crude held $72 range; natural gas firm on heat outlook offset by Qatar LNG supply signal.

outside_the_pit.title: Senate Farm Bill Omits E15, Prop 12, Pesticide Labeling
outside_the_pit.body: Senate Agriculture Chair John Boozman released the chamber's farm bill proposal Tuesday, making sweeping policy changes while leaving out Democrats' top priorities, setting up a partisan fight. The ASA says it is encouraged; the path to passage remains uncertain, and Senate ratification is not a given.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Commits $60 Million to Small Meat Processors
outside_the_pit.body: The United States Cattlemen's Association welcomed USDA's Small Processors Action Plan and its $60 million in new funding for meat and poultry processors. This is a structural play at packer concentration, a story the Cargill lockout has kept front of mind for cattle producers.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Advancing Northward Through Central America
outside_the_pit.body: A 2023 breach in Panama led to rapid northward spread of New World screwworm through Central America, and the fight at the border barrier requires constant vigilance. U.S. cattle producers near the southern border are watching this closely; an incursion would have immediate herd health and trade implications.

spread_to_watch.label: Corn nearby / Dec '26 carry spread
spread_to_watch.level: $0.27 carry, nearby at steep discount, widening sharply
spread_to_watch.commentary: The 27-cent gap between nearby corn at $4.11 and Dec '26 at $4.38 is the market's loudest signal today. Old-crop supply is ample enough that the nearby broke 6.2% while new crop held; that is not a broad grain event, it is a pipeline story. When pollination heat arrives and if it stresses the crop, watch this spread narrow fast as new crop reprices upward.

basis.headline: Corn basis diverging: Belt east firming, west softening further.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is firming as merchandisers move to capture spread value ahead of potential weather premium in new crop. **Producers in the eastern Corn Belt with bushels to move have a window that the futures board is not fully pricing yet.** Western Belt basis continues to soften, consistent with the seasonal pattern and an ample old-crop pipeline. The 27-cent carry between nearby and December is the backdrop: storage is not being paid, and basis is where the local signal lives right now.

weekly_thread.question: Does USDA Crop Progress confirm the Belt is ahead of pace, removing the last weather premium from corn?
weekly_thread.status_text: Monday's Crop Progress showed the Belt ahead of the 5-year average with no weather premium priced in. Today's nearby corn break confirms the market was already treating old-crop supply as ample. But the 6-to-10 day heat and humidity forecast arriving this morning just put the weather premium question back on the table for new crop, with pollination approaching.

the_more_you_know.title: The Carry Inversion: When the Nearby Price Tells You More Than the Chart
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 27-cent gap between nearby corn at $4.11 and Dec '26 at $4.38 is called a carry market, meaning the futures curve is paying you to wait. But here is what the spread is actually saying: when the nearby breaks hard and new crop holds, it is not a corn market selloff. It is the market separating two different problems. The nearby is pricing old-crop supply sitting in bins and elevators right now. New crop is pricing an unresolved growing season. The carry is wide because those two problems are not the same size today. When heat arrives during pollination and if it stresses the crop, the new-crop price moves up and the carry narrows fast, sometimes in a single session. The 27-cent carry is not a reward for storage right now. It is a signal about where the risk actually sits.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: corn under 400K MT keeps the nearby under pressure; above 500K MT would be the first real demand signal since the break.

Heat and humidity forecast for the Heartland: above-normal temps east of the New Mexico-to-Red River line arriving next week. If the forecast extends through pollination window, watch Dec '26 corn above $4.45 as the new-crop weather premium reentry point.

Cargill Fort Morgan and Schuyler lockout resolution: any settlement announcement resets the cattle complex immediately, watch live cattle above $248 as the first level that has to break for a recovery leg.
USDA Crop Progress: corn condition ratings below 65% good-to-excellent would confirm the heat is doing work. Above 68% and the weather premium stays priced out.
USDA Acreage Report: the planted acreage number for corn and beans this late in the season will either confirm the planting story or introduce a surprise that resets both contracts.

daily_quote.text: In the short run, the market is a voting machine but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices; USDA NASS Crop Progress; Brownfield Ag News; Feedstuffs; OilPrice.com; farmpolicynews.illinois.edu; beefmagazine.com; EIA
